speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're focusing on GitHub Mobile: what it is, how to get around it, and how to use it accessibly on iOS and Android.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this feels especially useful for people who don't always want to open a laptop just to check a review request or respond to an issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub Mobile is best understood as a companion workflow. It brings issues, pull requests, notifications, and code review to iPhone, iPad, and Android devices. For many learners, especially blind and low-vision contributors, it can be a very practical way to stay engaged when away from the desktop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this isn't, "replace your whole Git workflow with a phone."

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. It is not your full desktop environment. You are not doing heavy branch management, conflict resolution, or large-scale coding comfortably in the app. But for triage, quick reviews, comments, issue management, and keeping up with team activity, it can be excellent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we go farther, can we do a fast mental map of the app for different kinds of users?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For screen reader users, GitHub Mobile supports VoiceOver on iOS and TalkBack on Android natively. A common pattern is swipe right to move to the next element, swipe left to move back, and double tap to activate. The app is organized around five bottom tabs, and that tab bar is one of the main navigation landmarks. For low-vision users, the app respects system text sizing, supports dark mode, and generally presents information in simpler mobile layouts. One especially important example is pull request diffs: on mobile they appear in a simplified single-column form rather than the more visually dense desktop diff table. For sighted users, the same structure applies: bottom tabs, notification badges, quick actions by swiping notifications, and a mobile-optimized PR diff view.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So even though our audience here is blind and low vision, it's helpful to know the visual model too, because people collaborate across different access preferences.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: A quick administrative note. This appendix is a reference companion to the material on working with issues, and it's also closely connected to notifications workflows. And if you ever need the current source of truth, use GitHub Docs and the GitHub changelog, because mobile apps change over time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a really important reminder. A button name or gesture support detail may shift as the app evolves.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. So learn the concepts, then verify the latest details in the official documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with setup. Installing GitHub Mobile is straightforward. On iOS, you download GitHub from the App Store. On Android, you download it from Google Play.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then sign in with your GitHub account.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And when the app asks whether to enable notifications, say yes if you want mobile to be genuinely useful. Notifications are central to the whole experience. They let you know about review requests, mentions, issue updates, and other activity without repeatedly checking the website.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone installs the app but disables notifications, they may miss the app's biggest advantage.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's exactly it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Once you're in, the app is organized around five tabs at the bottom of the screen.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's name them slowly, because this is the core map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Home, Notifications, Explore, Pull Requests, and Profile. Home is your personalized activity feed across repositories. Notifications is where mentions, review requests, and issue updates appear. Explore helps you discover repositories and trending projects. Pull Requests collects PRs assigned to you, created by you, or waiting for your review. And Profile contains your own profile information, repositories, stars, and settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For a screen reader user, that tab bar becomes a repeated anchor point.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. On iOS with VoiceOver, you might swipe across the bottom to move between tabs, then double tap to activate. Sighted users may single tap directly. The important idea is that these five tabs define the app's main structure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone gets lost, returning to the tab bar can re-orient them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's turn to iOS and VoiceOver.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Starting with the basics: how do you turn VoiceOver on?

speaker: ALEX
text: Two common ways. You can triple-click the side button on newer iPhones, or the Home button on older devices, if that accessibility shortcut is configured. Or you can go through Settings, then Accessibility, then VoiceOver, and toggle it on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once VoiceOver is running, what gestures matter most inside GitHub Mobile?

speaker: ALEX
text: The essential set is: swipe right for next element, swipe left for previous element, double tap to activate the focused item, two-finger swipe up to read from the top, two-finger tap to pause or resume speech, three-finger swipe left or right to move between screens, and the two-finger scrub, that Z-shaped gesture, to go back.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one is especially nice on iOS because it gives you a quick escape route.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you've gone into a notification or opened a pull request and want to return, scrub can be very efficient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the rotor? Because that's one of the most powerful VoiceOver tools, and many learners underuse it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. In GitHub Mobile, the rotor can make navigation much faster. You open it by rotating two fingers on the screen as though turning a dial. Useful rotor settings here include Headings, Links, Form Controls, and Actions. Headings helps you jump between sections in an issue or pull request description. Links helps you move through linked issues, commits, or external URLs. Form Controls can take you directly to interactive fields, such as a comment box. And Actions can expose operations related to the focused element, such as assign, label, or close, when those actions are available.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a learner is swiping endlessly through a long PR description, the advice is: stop, use the rotor, switch to Headings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's one of the highest-value accessibility habits in the app.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if they want to comment?

speaker: ALEX
text: A typical VoiceOver workflow is: navigate to the comment field, either by swiping or by using the rotor set to Form Controls. Double tap to activate the field and bring up the keyboard. Type the comment, with VoiceOver announcing characters as you enter them. Then swipe until you reach the Comment button and double tap to submit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds simple, but it's worth stating because forms on mobile can feel disorienting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, especially when focus shifts unexpectedly or the keyboard changes the layout. A deliberate pattern helps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's look at Android with TalkBack.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you enable it?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can go to Settings, then Accessibility, then TalkBack, and toggle it on. Many Android devices also support a shortcut by holding both volume keys for three seconds, if that shortcut has been enabled.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the core gestures?

speaker: ALEX
text: Much like VoiceOver in spirit, though not identical in detail. Swipe right for the next element, swipe left for the previous one, double tap to activate. Swipe up then down to scroll down. Swipe down then up to scroll up. Swipe right then left to go back. And two-finger swipe down to read from the top.

speaker: JAMIE
text: One thing that helps learners is remembering that screen readers often share a navigation philosophy even when the exact gestures differ.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. Learn the pattern: move, activate, read continuously, go back, adjust granularity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That brings us to the TalkBack menu.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can open the TalkBack menu with a three-finger tap, or on some setups by swiping down then right. In that menu, you can do things like change reading granularity to character, word, line, or paragraph; access reading controls; and copy text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why does granularity matter in GitHub Mobile?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because technical text can be dense. If you're reviewing a pull request, reading an issue description, or trying to inspect a changed line, the ability to move by character or word can be essential. It gives you finer control than simply swiping element by element.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And writing a comment with TalkBack?

speaker: ALEX
text: The common pattern is: locate the comment field, which TalkBack may announce as something like "Edit text, double tap to edit." Double tap to enter the field. Type using the on-screen keyboard or dictation. Then locate the Comment button and double tap to submit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the iOS and Android workflows are parallel, even though the screen reader mechanics differ.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now to one of the strongest parts of GitHub Mobile: notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is where mobile can actually be better than the website for some accessibility users, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very often, yes. The Notifications tab presents activity in a clean, linear list. For assistive technology users, especially screen reader users, that can be much easier to navigate than a more crowded web page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's paint the picture.

speaker: ALEX
text: Each notification appears as an item in a chronological list. Unread items typically have an unread indicator. For sighted users, that may be a blue dot on the left. The row also includes useful context like repository name, notification type, and title. For screen reader users, swiping through the list one item at a time can make triage very manageable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are quick actions too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Swipe left on a notification to reveal actions such as Mark as read, Archive, and Unsubscribe. In some contexts, swiping right can mark a notification as done. Tapping the notification opens the underlying issue or pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does this translate for VoiceOver users, since swipe gestures can mean navigation rather than app-specific actions?

speaker: ALEX
text: Great question. On iOS, custom actions may be available through the VoiceOver Actions rotor item on each notification. So instead of relying purely on sighted-style swipe gestures, a VoiceOver user can focus the notification, switch the rotor to Actions, and then perform the available action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a very helpful clarification.

speaker: ALEX
text: Filtering is another important feature. At the top right of the Notifications tab, there is a filter control, often represented visually by a funnel icon. You can filter by type, such as issues, pull requests, or releases; by repository; and by reason, such as being mentioned, assigned, or asked to review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And with a screen reader?

speaker: ALEX
text: Those filters should appear as accessible form elements. Open the filter panel, navigate through the available controls, and toggle the criteria you want. This lets you narrow a busy inbox to the most relevant activity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone says, "My notifications are overwhelming," the answer is not just archive more. It's also filter intelligently.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Filter by reason, filter by repository, and use quick actions aggressively.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's move into pull requests. Mobile is well suited to quick PR review: checking status, reading the description, scanning changed files, and leaving an approval or short comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But maybe not a forty-file architectural review on a phone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Use the right tool for the task. When you open a pull request in GitHub Mobile, you'll generally encounter a few main sections: the Description, the Commits list, Files changed, and Checks for CI or other status information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And these are often announced as headings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That's important. VoiceOver users can use the rotor set to Headings to jump among those sections. TalkBack users can use heading navigation or reading granularity methods available on their device. That means you don't have to swipe through every single element in sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk through leaving a review.

speaker: ALEX
text: A typical flow is: find the Review changes button, activate it, choose whether you want to Approve, Request changes, or Comment, optionally add text in the review comment field, and then activate Submit review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the review is not just a comment; it's a structured decision.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Approve means the changes look good. Request changes signals that the author should revise something before merge. Comment lets you leave feedback without explicitly approving or blocking.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And viewing diffs on mobile has a distinct accessibility advantage.

speaker: ALEX
text: It often does. In the Files Changed area, GitHub Mobile uses a simplified diff. Additions and removals are shown as text rather than forcing you through a two-column desktop-style table. Changed lines may be announced in a direct way, such as "Added" followed by content, or "Removed" followed by content.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That can be easier to understand with a screen reader than a dense side-by-side layout.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. On a small screen, the simpler structure can actually improve access.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's talk about issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is one of the most practical mobile tasks: filing something when you notice it, instead of telling yourself you'll remember later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. To file an issue, you generally navigate to the repository, choose Issues, then find the plus button or New Issue. Enter the title and body. Optionally set labels, assignees, and a milestone if those are part of the workflow. Then submit the issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Even if the source text was truncated there, the workflow is clear: repository, issues, new issue, fill in fields, optionally set metadata, submit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And those optional fields matter in team workflows. Labels classify the issue, assignees indicate responsibility, and milestones connect the issue to a broader goal or release.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about finding issues that matter to you?

speaker: ALEX
text: In mobile, you can use your notifications, repository issue lists, and your personal areas in the app to return to issues you've opened, been assigned, or been mentioned in. The core idea is to combine repository navigation with notification-driven access, rather than hunting manually every time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So for many learners, notifications become the front door to issue work too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so.

speaker: ALEX
text: At this point it's worth stepping back and asking: what does mobile do well, and what should remain a desktop task?

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make that explicit.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Mobile does well at staying current. Notifications, quick triage, lightweight issue filing, short comments, straightforward PR reviews, and checking checks or CI status are all good mobile tasks. The interface is linear, compact, and often easier for assistive technology users than a more cluttered desktop page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And desktop still wins for deep work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you need to read a very large pull request, compare lots of files, manage complicated issue templates, perform substantial code editing, resolve merge conflicts, or do anything that depends on a full Git client and development environment, desktop is the better choice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So mobile is not lesser. It's specialized.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Think of it as the right tool for quick response and continuous awareness.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's finish with common issues and workarounds, because these practical frustrations matter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: First: VoiceOver skips some pull request descriptions.

speaker: ALEX
text: A good workaround is to switch the rotor to Headings or Links and navigate by structure instead of plain sequential swiping. If the description contains complex formatting, try moving by headings, then by links, and explore the content in smaller pieces. If necessary, reopen the item or use another navigation mode rather than assuming the content is absent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Second: TalkBack does not announce the new notifications badge.

speaker: ALEX
text: In that case, don't rely only on the badge. Open the Notifications tab directly and review the list. Mobile badges can be inconsistent across apps and devices, so the reliable method is to check the tab itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Third: the keyboard covers the comment field.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is a common mobile problem. After activating the comment field, you may need to scroll slightly, dismiss and reopen the keyboard, rotate the device if your setup allows, or move focus carefully until the submit control comes back into view. On either platform, patience and incremental navigation help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Fourth: after writing a review, you can't find the Submit button.

speaker: ALEX
text: This often means focus is still trapped near the keyboard or text field. Try navigating past the editing area deliberately. On iOS, rotor settings like Form Controls can help jump among interactive elements. On Android, move through controls methodically or adjust granularity if needed. If the button still doesn't appear, dismiss the keyboard and continue exploring the screen.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And fifth: the app crashes or freezes.

speaker: ALEX
text: The practical steps are the usual ones: force close and reopen the app, make sure it's updated, and if the problem persists, sign out and sign back in through Profile and Settings. If you still cannot complete the task, switch to the web or desktop workflow so your work is not blocked.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's an important accessibility principle in itself: have a fallback path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Accessibility isn't only about one perfect interface. It's also about knowing alternate routes when a tool becomes unreliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we close with a compact recap?

speaker: ALEX
text: Certainly. GitHub Mobile gives you five main tabs for navigation: Home, Notifications, Explore, Pull Requests, and Profile. On iOS, VoiceOver users rely on familiar gestures and especially the rotor, with Headings, Form Controls, Links, and Actions being especially valuable. On Android, TalkBack users use core swipe and activation gestures, plus the TalkBack menu for reading granularity and controls. Notifications are one of the strongest mobile features, because they provide a clean linear inbox with filtering and quick actions. Pull request review works well for lightweight review, comments, and approvals, with a simplified diff view that can be more accessible than desktop on a small screen. Issues can be filed and managed on mobile, especially for quick capture and response. And throughout all of this, mobile is best seen as a companion to desktop rather than a full replacement.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if learners want the latest details, they should check GitHub Docs, the GitHub Mobile docs specifically, and the GitHub changelog.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Official references matter, because apps evolve.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That was a practical one.

speaker: ALEX
text: It was. And for many contributors, mastering mobile means staying connected to open source and team workflows in the moments between full desktop sessions.
